verdict_reasoning: B4GALT1 (β-1,4-galactosyltransferase-1) is canonically a Golgi-resident trans-Golgi-network (TGN) enzyme with a type II transmembrane topology (N-terminal cytoplasmic tail, TM anchor, C-terminal catalytic luminal domain). However, B4GALT1 has well-documented cell-surface localization in specialized contexts: (1) documented PM cycling and dual localization — the protein transits between the TGN and PM via secretory vesicles in certain cell types; (2) tissue/cell-type restriction — surface display is enriched in particular cell lineages (notably neurons and some immune cells) where it participates in surface glycosylation; (3) activation-induced display — immune-cell activation can relocalize B4GALT1 to the PM where it functions as a juxtacrine glycosyltransferase. The bulk glycosylation literature emphasizes TGN function, but the documented PM pool and activation-inducible surface residence qualify this as dual-localization contextual surface accessibility rather than baseline yes.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Whether the baseline PM residence is constitutive but minor (classic dual-localization) vs. primarily activation-induced and cell-type-restricted. Literature emphasizes TGN/Golgi; PM-proteomics and cell-surface-biotinylation evidence for B4GALT1 is less extensively documented than for paradigm surface recyclers, creating ambiguity between the strength of the PM pool and its functional relevance as a surface target.
